rationale_for_baseline_tier: Acute arterial occlusion of the left lower limb with sensorimotor deficit (complete anaesthesia and foot drop), a pulseless perishing-cold pale/mottled limb, and a rigid tender calf — an immediately threatened / advanced limb (Rutherford IIb–III). The viability window for salvage is measured in hours and the patient is a bedbound, high-dependency aged-care resident who cannot self-transport. This mandates an emergency (Priority 1) ambulance to a vascular-capable hospital — T5, call 000 — exactly as the source clinician arranged. Bypassing after-hours triage to wait for the morning GP round would push the patient beyond the salvage window and commit them to major amputation.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A suddenly cold, pale/mottled, painful leg with numbness and inability to move the foot (with or without an absent Doppler pulse)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Call 000 immediately — the limb-salvage window is only a few hours
escalation_edges.rationale: The six-P constellation with neurological deficit is a vascular emergency; only emergency revascularisation can save the limb, so an emergency ambulance is required at once.
escalation_edges.communication_template: This is an emergency — the blood supply to the leg is blocked. Call 000 now for an ambulance and ask for a hospital with vascular surgery. While you wait, keep the leg hanging down, do not raise it, do not put heat or bandages on it, and stay with him.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Worsening before the ambulance arrives — spreading numbness/paralysis, the limb turning dusky/black or blistering, tense skin, or the severe pain suddenly giving way to painlessness
escalation_edges.rationale: These signal an advancing or non-viable limb and possible reperfusion/metabolic risk; the emergency response must continue and paramedics/000 be updated — do not stand down.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the leg gets more numb, can't move at all, turns dark or blisters, or the pain suddenly stops, tell 000 straight away and keep the ambulance coming — do not wait.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Patient becomes drowsy, collapses, or stops responding while waiting
escalation_edges.rationale: Systemic deterioration (from pain, cardiovascular events, or reperfusion once flow returns) requires immediate resuscitation-capable response; keep the patient flat if collapsed and stay on the line with 000.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he becomes very drowsy or collapses, lay him down, keep 000 on the line and follow their instructions — start CPR if he stops breathing normally and you have been shown how.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Frail, bedbound, high-dependency aged-care resident (~78) with peripheral arterial disease
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Frailty and immobility do NOT lower the tier — limb salvage still depends on emergency revascularisation. Self-transport is not viable; an emergency ambulance is mandatory. The receiving hospital should be told of the aged-care context and existing medications for the vascular team's decision-making.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Facility nursing staff are present and are the ones executing the advice
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Give clear, specific carer-actionable instructions (keep dependent, no elevation/heat/compression, stay with the patient, prepare transfer documents and unlock access) and confirm 000 has been called.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 now for an emergency ambulance and ask for a hospital with vascular surgery — the blood supply to the leg is blocked and this is a surgical emergency. While waiting: keep the affected leg hanging down over the side of the bed (dependent); do NOT elevate it, do NOT apply heat packs, and do NOT use compression stockings. Keep the patient still and calm, stay with them, and have their medication list and transfer documents ready for the paramedics.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 operates 24 hours a day — this is a time-critical emergency and must not wait for the morning GP round or clinic hours.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: The hospital will provide a discharge/operative summary; the nursing home physician should be notified of the transfer and the vascular team informed of the peripheral arterial disease history and current antiplatelet/statin therapy.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Anything below an emergency ambulance — advising a GP visit, outpatient DVT ultrasound, self-transport to ED, or at-home pain management — fails to treat a time-critical limb-threatening arterial occlusion.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Delay beyond the few-hour salvage window results in irreversible ischaemia, gangrene and major amputation, and reperfusion of dead muscle can cause fatal hyperkalaemia.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Directing the patient to same-day urgent care or to self-transport to ED under-recognises the need for a Priority 1 ambulance in a bedbound patient and loses critical time.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: T5 is the correct tier here, so over-triage is not a meaningful risk in this case; the danger is entirely under-triage.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether the limb is still salvageable (Rutherford IIb) or already irreversible (III), and the exact level/mechanism of occlusion, cannot be determined via telehealth.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat as a salvageable emergency and escalate immediately — the vascular team resolves salvageability in hospital. Uncertainty raises urgency, it does not lower it.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Using the uncertainty (or the patient's frailty) to justify a wait-and-see, outpatient, or GP pathway.
